description: You've already started a security-reviewing multi-agent coding tool on Groq's free tier with MCP-based tool use, and did Kaggle's 5-Day AI Agents Intensive — so agent architecture and MCP tool-use basics aren't zero-knowledge. This roadmap deepens the concepts (memory, planning/reasoning loops) and treats frameworks as things to understand and compare against your own from-scratch implementation, not necessarily to adopt wholesale, given your stated preference for building things yourself.

topics.title: Agent Architecture
topics.note: You have a working mental model from your own multi-agent tool — this formalizes it and fills gaps.

subtopics.title: Core agent loop: perceive → reason → act → observe, and how this maps onto your security-review agent's control flow
Single-agent vs multi-agent architectures: orchestrator/worker patterns, when multiple agents actually help vs add coordination overhead
Role specialization in your own tool: formalize planner/coder/security-reviewer agent boundaries and handoff protocol
resources: Self-directed design doc on your existing project

subtopics.title: Failure modes: infinite loops, hallucinated tool args, cascading errors across agents — and guardrails against each
Human-in-the-loop patterns: approval gates for risky actions (relevant for a security-review tool that might flag/fix code)

topics.title: Tool Usage
topics.note: You already use MCP-based tool use — this deepens reliability and design, since basics are covered.

subtopics.title: Tool schema design: how tight/loose parameter definitions affect call reliability
MCP protocol deep dive beyond what you've explored: resources, prompts primitives (not just tools), server lifecycle
Writing your own MCP server for a custom tool (e.g., a code-linting/security-scan tool for your agent)
Tool call validation & sandboxing: preventing a coding agent from executing unsafe commands unchecked
resources: Self-directed, directly relevant to a security-reviewing agent's own safety

subtopics.title: Parallel tool calls and dependency ordering (when Tool B needs Tool A's result first)

topics.title: Memory Systems
topics.note: Genuinely new territory — your current tool likely doesn't have persistent memory yet.

subtopics.title: Short-term memory: context window management, conversation summarization to avoid overflow during long agent runs
Long-term memory: vector-store-backed episodic memory (ties directly to your embeddings/vector DB learning)
resources: MemGPT paper (Packer et al.) for the core concepts

subtopics.title: Working memory / scratchpad patterns for multi-step reasoning within a single task
Cross-session memory: giving your security-review agent persistent knowledge of a codebase across multiple runs
resources: Self-directed project, store findings/context in pgvector or Redis

subtopics.title: Memory retrieval strategies: recency vs relevance weighting, avoiding stale/contradictory memory retrieval

topics.title: Planning & Reasoning Loops
topics.note: The part that turns 'an LLM calling tools' into an actual agent — likely your biggest gap right now.

subtopics.title: Task decomposition: breaking 'review this PR for security issues' into a concrete subtask plan
Self-reflection/critique loops (Reflexion-style): agent reviews its own output before finalizing
Tree-of-thought / multi-path exploration for harder reasoning tasks vs a single linear chain
resources: Tree of Thoughts paper (Yao et al.)

subtopics.title: Termination conditions: knowing when a security review is 'done' vs looping indefinitely for more issues
resources: Self-directed design exercise on your own tool

subtopics.title: Implement a planner-executor loop in your multi-agent tool: explicit plan step, execution step, re-plan-on-failure step
resources: Self-directed project on your existing codebase

topics.title: Frameworks: LangChain, LlamaIndex, AutoGen
topics.note: Given your stated no-abstraction philosophy, treat these as things to read/evaluate critically rather than default to — understand what they automate so you can judge whether to use them or keep building from scratch.

subtopics.title: LangChain fundamentals: chains, agents, LCEL — and honestly assess what it buys you vs your from-scratch approach
LlamaIndex fundamentals: primarily for RAG/data-connector patterns — compare against your own pgvector-based RAG build
AutoGen fundamentals: multi-agent conversation patterns, group chat orchestration — directly comparable to your own multi-agent tool's design
Build the same small agent task three ways (from scratch, LangChain, AutoGen) and write up tradeoffs — informs whether to adopt any framework long-term

topics.title: What to Build: Coding agent, Search agent, Autonomous task agent
topics.note: Your coding agent is already in progress — these extend it and add two new agent types.

subtopics.title: Push your security-reviewing coding agent to a real milestone: planning loop + memory + sandboxed tool execution integrated
Build a search agent: query decomposition, web search tool calls, source aggregation, citation-aware summarization
Build an autonomous task agent: given a high-level goal, it plans, executes multi-step tool calls, and self-corrects without a human in every loop iteration
Add evaluation harness across all three agents: success rate, number of steps, failure taxonomy
resources: Self-directed, ties into prompt-eval work from AI Engineering domain
